prompt: What is a Network Attached Storage (NAS)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a storage system directly attached to a server or workstation

choices.label: B
choices.text: a computer connected to a network that provides only file-based data storage services to other devices

choices.label: C
choices.text: a remote storage unit connected to a PC using a specific networking technology

choices.label: D
choices.text: a computer connected to a network that provides computation to other devices

solution: From the DAS/NAS/SAN lecture, a **NAS** is a dedicated **file-level** storage device (essentially a specialized computer/appliance) attached to a TCP/IP network that serves files to clients over file-sharing protocols such as **NFS/SMB**. Option A describes **DAS** (Direct-Attached Storage); option C with a 'specific networking technology' (block-level over Fibre Channel / iSCSI) describes a **SAN**; option D describes a compute server, not storage. NAS is therefore B: file-based storage shared over the network.

prompt: Downtime is a period of time, or a percentage of a time span, when a system is unavailable or offline __________.
solution: In the Dependability lecture, **downtime** (which determines availability $A=\text{uptime}/(\text{uptime}+\text{downtime})$) is any period during which the system is not delivering correct service / is unavailable, **regardless of the cause** — whether planned maintenance, crashes, power loss, etc. It is not limited to maintenance (B) or crashes (C) alone. Hence D is correct.

prompt: In a three-layer network architecture of a data center, which is the layer that is typically associated with the TOR (Top-of-Rack) switch?
solution: In the classic three-tier datacenter network (access / aggregation / core), the **access** layer connects directly to the servers. The Top-of-Rack (TOR) switch sits at the top of each rack and aggregates the rack's server NICs, so it is an **access-layer** switch. Aggregation interconnects access switches; core is the high-speed backbone. Hence A.

prompt: Which definition is **not** encompassed by Dependability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reversibility: ability to reverse a broken service

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintainability: reparation to restore correct service

solution: Dependability (Dependability lecture) is an umbrella concept comprising attributes such as **reliability** (continuity of correct service — B), **availability** (readiness for correct service — D), **maintainability** (ability to undergo repairs/restore correct service — C), plus safety, integrity, confidentiality. **'Reversibility'** is **not** one of the dependability attributes — it is a made-up/extra term. Hence A is the definition not encompassed by dependability.

subStatements.motivation: From the Servers / datacenter building lecture, a 19-inch rack is populated with rack-units that hold not only servers but also networking equipment — notably the **Top-of-Rack (TOR) switch** and other switches — as well as patch panels, PDUs, etc. So a rack's shelves/units may indeed contain switches. The statement is True.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Racks host servers and networking gear including TOR switches — the statement is **True** (official answer-box A, where box A reads 'True').

prompt: In the context of SSDs, Blocks are the smallest unit that can be read/written.

subStatements.statement: In the context of SSDs, Blocks are the smallest unit that can be read/written.
subStatements.motivation: From the SSD/flash lecture, NAND flash is organized into **blocks** which are made of **pages**. The **page** is the smallest unit that can be **read or written (programmed)**, whereas the **block** is the smallest unit that can be **erased**. So blocks are the erase granularity, not the read/write granularity — the statement is False (it confuses block with page).

solution: See per-statement motivation. In SSDs the **page** is the read/write unit and the **block** is the erase unit — the statement is **False** (official answer-box B, where box B reads 'False').

prompt: Paravirtualization is a particular type of kernel-level virtualization that does not rely on a host OS.

subStatements.statement: Paravirtualization is a particular type of kernel-level virtualization that does not rely on a host OS.
subStatements.motivation: From the Virtualization lecture, **paravirtualization** is a (hypervisor-based) virtualization technique in which the guest OS is modified to issue hypercalls to the VMM — it is not 'kernel-level virtualization'. 'Kernel-level virtualization that does not rely on a host OS' is a mischaracterization; the defining feature of paravirtualization is the modified guest interface, not the absence of a host OS, and the description conflates distinct categories. The statement is False.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Paravirtualization is defined by a modified guest OS issuing hypercalls, not by being 'kernel-level virtualization without a host OS' — the statement is **False** (official answer-box A, where box A reads 'False').

prompt: The structure (or topology) of a reliability block diagram is always the same as the structure (or topology) of the modeled system.

subStatements.statement: The structure (or topology) of a reliability block diagram is always the same as the structure (or topology) of the modeled system.
subStatements.motivation: From the Dependability lecture, a **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)** models the **logical/functional** dependency of correct operation on the components, **not** the physical/topological layout. The RBD topology can differ from the physical structure: e.g. components physically in parallel may be a **series** RBD if both are required for the function (and vice-versa). The classic example is a bridge/parallel-redundant layout whose RBD does not mirror the wiring. Hence the statement is False.

solution: See per-statement motivation. The RBD captures the logical success path, which need not match the physical topology — the statement is **False** (official answer-box A, where box A reads 'False').

prompt: A system virtual machine provides a complete system environment that can support an operating system (potentially with many user processes).

subStatements.statement: A system virtual machine provides a complete system environment that can support an operating system (potentially with many user processes).
subStatements.motivation: From the Virtualization lecture, a **system virtual machine** provides a complete, persistent **system environment** that virtualizes a full set of hardware resources, so it **can run an entire guest operating system** together with its many user processes. This is contrasted with a **process virtual machine**, which supports only a single process/application. The statement is the textbook definition of a system VM and is True.

solution: See per-statement motivation. A system VM virtualizes full hardware and can host a complete OS with many processes — the statement is **True** (official answer-box B, where box B reads 'True').

prompt: A scientific computation uses a server composed of 2 CPUs and one TPU. Knowing that: the computation requires 12 days to complete; the computation requires both CPUs **and** the TPU within the server to be properly working; $MTTF_{CPU}=80\,\text{days}$ and $MTTF_{TPU}=40\,\text{days}$. How many instances of the computation should be executed to have a probability of $98\%$ to have at least one instance producing the results? (Use at least 4 decimal digits; all other components are ideal.)
solution:
**Reliability of one server over the $t=12$ day run** (exponential, all required components in **series**):
$$R_{CPU}(12)=e^{-12/80}=e^{-0.15}=0.8607,\qquad R_{TPU}(12)=e^{-12/40}=e^{-0.30}=0.7408.$$
$$R_{server}=R_{CPU}^2\cdot R_{TPU}=0.8607^2\times 0.7408=0.7408\times 0.7408\approx 0.5488.$$

**Run $n$ independent instances**; the probability that at least one completes is $1-(1-R_{server})^n$. Require it $\ge 0.98$:
$$(1-0.5488)^n\le 0.02 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.4512^{\,n}\le 0.02$$
$$n\ge \frac{\ln 0.02}{\ln 0.4512}=\frac{-3.912}{-0.7958}=4.915.$$

Round up: $\boxed{n=5\ \text{instances}.}$

prompt: The PoliMi data center has two simultaneously active (redundant) cooling distribution systems. Knowing that the availability of the cooling system should be $99.99\%$ and the MTTF of a single distribution system is 150 days, what is the maximum MTTR needed to reach the required availability value? (Use at least 4 decimal digits.)
solution:
Two redundant distribution systems form a **parallel** system; the cooling is unavailable only if **both** fail. Required system availability $A_{sys}=0.9999$:
$$A_{sys}=1-(1-A)^2 \;\Rightarrow\; (1-A)^2 = 1-0.9999 = 0.0001 \;\Rightarrow\; 1-A = 0.01 \;\Rightarrow\; A \ge 0.99.$$

Each single system must have availability $A=\dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF+MTTR}\ge 0.99$. Solve for the maximum $MTTR$ with $MTTF=150$ days:
$$MTTR\le MTTF\cdot\frac{1-A}{A}=150\times\frac{0.01}{0.99}=150\times 0.010101.$$

$$\boxed{MTTR \approx 1.515\ \text{days}.}$$

prompt: Consider an HDD with a data transfer rate of $20\,\text{MB/s}$, a rotation speed of $10000\,\text{RPM}$, a mean seek time of $6\,\text{ms}$, and a negligible controller overhead. What is the minimum locality required to achieve a mean I/O service time of $4.45\,\text{ms}$ to transfer a sector of $2\,\text{KB}$?
solution:
**Average rotational latency** (half a revolution at $10000$ RPM):
$$T_{rot}=\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{60}{10000}\,\text{s}=3\,\text{ms}.$$

**Transfer time** for one $2\,\text{KB}$ sector at $20\,\text{MB/s}$:
$$T_{transfer}=\frac{2\,\text{KB}}{20\,\text{MB/s}}=\frac{2/1024\,\text{MB}}{20\,\text{MB/s}}\approx 0.0977\,\text{ms}.$$

**I/O service-time model with locality** (the seek + rotation are paid only for the non-local fraction $(1-\text{loc})$):
$$T_{I/O}=T_{transfer}+(1-\text{loc})\,(T_{seek}+T_{rot}).$$
Impose $T_{I/O}=4.45\,\text{ms}$ and solve for the locality:
$$1-\text{loc}=\frac{4.45-0.0977}{6+3}=\frac{4.3523}{9}=0.4836 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{loc}=0.5164.$$

$$\boxed{\text{loc}\approx 51.64\%.}$$

prompt: Consider a RAID 6 configuration with 10 disks. What is the minimum number of I/O operations requested to update one block of a data disk (considering the sum over the entire set of disks)?
solution:
RAID 6 keeps **two** independent parity blocks ($P$ and $Q$) per stripe. To update **one** data block using the read-modify-write method, the controller must:

- **read** the old data block (1)
- **read** the old parity $P$ (1)
- **read** the old parity $Q$ (1)
- **write** the new data block (1)
- **write** the new parity $P$ (1)
- **write** the new parity $Q$ (1)

Total $=3$ reads $+3$ writes $=\boxed{6\ \text{I/O operations}}$ (independent of the number of disks, which is why the count is the same for 10 disks).

prompt: Consider the following RAID 0+1 setup considering 6 disks, each one with an MTTF equal to 600 days and an MTTR equal to 5 days. Consider a single mirror case for the RAID 1 part. What is the MTTF of the storage infrastructure?
solution:
**RAID 0+1** with $m=6$ disks = a **mirror (RAID 1)** of two **striped (RAID 0)** groups of 3 disks each.

**Step 1 — MTTF of one RAID 0 group** (3 disks in series): $$MTTF_{RAID0}=\frac{MTTF_{disk}}{n}=\frac{600}{3}=200\,\text{days}$$

**Step 2 — mirror the two groups** (2-way mirror with repair $MTTR=5$ days): $$MTTF_{0+1}=\frac{MTTF_{group}^2}{2\cdot MTTR}=\frac{200^2}{2\cdot 5}=4000\,\text{days}$$

Equivalently $MTTF_{0+1}=\dfrac{2\,MTTF_{disk}^2}{m^2\,MTTR}=\dfrac{2\cdot 600^2}{36\cdot 5}=4000$ days.

**FINAL RESULT:** $MTTF\approx\mathbf{4000\ days}\ (\approx 10.96\ \text{years})$.

prompt: Your system initially includes one CPU and one disk and serves 30 users characterised by 20s think time. The CPU demanding time is $D_{CPU}=100\,\text{ms}$ while the disk demanding time is $D_{DISK}=300\,\text{ms}$. How many disks do you need to install in your system so that the response-time lower bound is lower than $500\,\text{ms}$? (Hint: assume you can evenly split the disk demand among all the disks you are going to use.)
solution:
Closed interactive network, $N=30$ users, $Z=20\,\text{s}$. With $k$ disks the disk demand is split evenly, so each disk station has demand $D_{DISK}/k$; the total demand $\sum D = D_{CPU}+D_{DISK}=0.1+0.3=0.4\,\text{s}$ is unchanged.

**Response-time lower bound** for an interactive system:
$$R(N)\ge \max\Big(\textstyle\sum D,\; N\,D_{max}-Z\Big).$$

Even with **one** disk ($k=1$, $D_{max}=D_{DISK}=0.3\,\text{s}$):
$$N\,D_{max}-Z = 30\times 0.3 - 20 = 9-20 = -11\,\text{s}\;(<0),$$
so the bound collapses to $R(N)\ge \sum D = 0.4\,\text{s}=400\,\text{ms}$, which is already $<500\,\text{ms}$.

The large think time keeps the system far from the saturation/bottleneck regime, so the lower bound is dominated by $\sum D$ regardless of the number of disks.

$$\boxed{\text{The single disk is already sufficient — no additional disks are needed (1 disk).}}$$

prompt: Consider the same system and situation as in the previous question. Knowing that the system response time $R_{sys}=30\,\text{s}$, what is the system throughput $X$?
solution:
For a closed **interactive** system, the Response-Time Law gives
$$R=\frac{N}{X}-Z \;\Longleftrightarrow\; X=\frac{N}{R+Z}.$$

With $N=30$ users, $R_{sys}=30\,\text{s}$, $Z=20\,\text{s}$:
$$X=\frac{30}{30+20}=\frac{30}{50}=\boxed{0.6\ \text{job/s}.}$$

prompt: Describe the write-amplification problem in the context of SSDs.
solution:
**Write amplification** is the phenomenon in flash-based SSDs where the **amount of data physically written to the NAND flash is larger than the amount of data the host actually requested to write**. It is quantified by the **Write Amplification Factor (WAF)**:
$$\text{WAF}=\frac{\text{data written to flash}}{\text{data written by the host}}\ge 1.$$

**Why it happens — the read/write/erase asymmetry of NAND flash:**
- Flash is organized into **blocks** composed of **pages**. Reads and writes (programming) happen at **page** granularity, but **erasing happens only at the whole-block granularity**.
- A page that already holds data **cannot be overwritten in place**: it must first be erased, but erasure works on the entire block. So updates are written **out-of-place** to a free page and the old page is just marked **invalid (stale)**.

**The mechanism that amplifies writes:**
- Over time blocks accumulate a mix of valid and invalid pages. To reclaim space, the **garbage collection** process must pick a victim block, **copy its still-valid pages elsewhere**, and then erase the whole block.
- Those copy operations are **extra physical writes not requested by the host**, so a single host write can trigger several internal page writes — this is write amplification.
- **Wear leveling** (spreading writes evenly so no block wears out prematurely) adds further internal data movement, also contributing to amplification.

**Why it matters:**
- **Endurance:** flash cells tolerate only a limited number of program/erase (P/E) cycles. Write amplification consumes those cycles faster, **shortening the SSD lifetime**.
- **Performance:** extra internal writes and erase-before-write cycles add latency and reduce sustained write throughput.

**Mitigations:** over-provisioning (keeping spare capacity to ease garbage collection), the TRIM command (letting the OS tell the SSD which pages are invalid so they need not be copied), efficient garbage-collection and wear-leveling algorithms, and write-combining/buffering. The goal is to keep the WAF as close to 1 as possible to preserve both endurance and performance.
